title: Health Visitor
how_to_become: To become a health visitor, you must be registered with the Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) as a nurse or midwife, then apply for an approved health visitor training programme.
description: Health visitors are registered nurses or midwives who have undertaken further study at master's level to become registered health visitors. They work in the community to provide a proactive and universal service to families with young children, aiming to support and empower families to promote health and wellbeing.
role_overview: Health visitors visit families in their own home, provide health advice and support based on the universal health visiting pathway: promoting and safeguarding children's wellbeing, building strong relationships with families from early in pregnancy, supporting early parenthood, and harnessing family strengths while assessing and responding to their needs.

entry_requirements.overview: You must first become a registered nurse or midwife, then apply for an approved health visitor training programme. To gain entry, you should be educated to degree level, though some universities accept prior learning.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to university, college, or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.prerequisite: Registration with the NMC as a nurse or midwife is required before applying for health visitor training.
education_and_training.college_route: HNC in Healthcare Practice can lead to an undergraduate pre-registration nursing or midwifery degree.
education_and_training.widening_access: SWAP supports adult learners with few or no qualifications to enter higher education.
education_and_training.qualification: Postgraduate programme in Specialist Community Public Health Nursing — Health Visiting.

learning_and_development.overview: Training is provided during induction and throughout your career in areas including child protection, advanced practice, teaching and supervision, and management and leadership.
learning_and_development.revalidation: Health visitors must revalidate with the NMC every 3 years.

progression_roles: Leadership roles via the NHS Leadership Academy

professional_bodies: Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) — mandatory registration

chunks.text: To become a health visitor, you must be NMC-registered as a nurse or midwife, then apply for an approved health visitor training programme (Specialist Community Public Health Nursing — Health Visiting postgraduate programme).

Health visitors are registered nurses or midwives with further master's-level study, working in the community to provide a proactive and universal service to families with young children, supporting and empowering families to promote health and wellbeing.

Health visitors visit families at home, promoting and safeguarding children's wellbeing, building strong relationships from early pregnancy, supporting early parenthood, and harnessing family strengths while assessing their needs.

Useful school subjects include Human Biology, Chemistry, English, Maths, and Psychology. NMC registration as a nurse or midwife is required before applying. Degree-level education is required, though prior learning may be considered.

Health visitor training is offered as postgraduate programmes in Specialist Community Public Health Nursing — Health Visiting at Glasgow Caledonian University, Queen Margaret University, Robert Gordon University, University of Stirling, and University of West of Scotland.
Health visitors can progress to team leader, team manager, specialist health visitor, or leadership roles via the NHS Leadership Academy.
